data.title: It's Once Again Cool to Walk the Golf CourseJournal Report
data.description:
By Joe Passov

The old cliché about golf being a "good walk spoiled" has long rankled golfers. It wasn't only a slur on a beloved game, but was also increasingly an anachronism, as more people opted for golf carts than actually walked.

These days, though, at least the walking part rings true. Walking, it seems, is making a comeback on the golf course.

In recent years, a number of high-profile, high-end public facilities have chosen to prohibit riding carts, looking to provide golfers with the "classic golf experience," which meant walking; reservations at several of these destinations are sold out 18 months in advance. Combine that with escalating sales of ultralightweight golf bags and power pushcarts — which are geared specifically to walking golfers — and a pandemic and postpandemic desire for outdoor exercise, and it's clear that walking has become cool once again.

"Not only is walking good for you," says Tim Miles, a 68-year-old retired law-enforcement officer in Scottsdale, Ariz., "but it's the camaraderie and the enjoyment of sharing a walking experience. That's why I play the game."

The percentage of rounds that are walked among "core golfers" — those who report playing eight or more rounds in a year — rose to 35% in 2021, the most-recent year available, from 31% in 2020, according to the National Golf Foundation. The West and the Northeast had the highest percentage of walking rounds, at 44% each, with the Midwest at 37% and the South at 21%. Clearly, the weather plays a role.

The number of walkers likely has continued to rise in recent years, says Greg Nathan, president and chief executive of the golf foundation. (Carts are generally banned on the pro tour.)

The decline of walking

Before 1950, pretty much all golfers walked. But in 1951, the first electric golf carts were sold, and by the end of the decade, those three- and four-wheeled electric "golf cars" were ubiquitous, joined by gasoline-powered models.

It didn't take long for walking to be out, and riding to be in. And golf courses adjusted. At Desert Mountain in Scottsdale, for instance, the six sprawling Jack Nicklaus-designed championship courses were designed beginning in 1987 with riding carts in mind. Not only were those layouts part of a residential community, which means the golf footprint is expanded to accommodate homesites, but the terrain itself — rolling, boulder-and cactus-studded terrain — would seemingly discourage walking.

Yet now, even at Desert Mountain, golf on foot is trending up.

"Our new members especially like to walk," says John Lyberger, director of golf at Desert Mountain. "They do want exercise. That's why I ordered 40 new power pushcarts a few months ago. We've definitely seen an increase in the amount of people that enjoy the game by walking."

Indeed, during a 2024 restoration of Desert Mountain's Cochise course, small changes were made to benefit walkers. "We formalized green-to-next-tee trails that the members had roughed out naturally," says Lyberger. "We made them a little wider, cleaned them up, made them look more pronounced so that it didn't look like it was something the deer were running through. We made a number of those adjustments to make the golf course more walker-friendly."

And it isn't just Scottsdale. "We're seeing it across the brand," says Spencer Cody, corporate director of club and golf operations for Omni Hotels & Resorts. "At the Omni Barton Creek in Austin, as the average age of the membership has declined and younger families are moving in, we're seeing at least a double-digit increase in walking rounds."

Heart healthy

The benefits to walking the golf course include the obvious and the not-so-obvious. According to a 2006 Mayo Clinic study, walking a regulation-length golf course yielded an average of 11,948 steps per 18-hole round of golf. More recent findings from smartwatch data suggests that a golfer can take some 14,000 steps on more-challenging courses and as many as 17,000 steps at resort courses in Pinehurst, N.C.

A 2022 study found that walkers averaged 99 heartbeats a minute, versus 90 for walkers using a power pushcart and 83 for those in a riding cart. The study also concluded that walkers burned more calories, had sharper mental focus and shot lower scores.

"From the time you hit the tee shot to get to the second shot, you're calculating as you're walking," says Lyberger. "It's not simply drive up and hit. You're sharing a conversation with three other people, not just the one you're riding with. When you're in a cart, you often zip by all the beauty. When you're walking, you can focus longer on your surroundings. It creates more of a serene, joyful atmosphere."

Walking golfers have benefited from updated, state-of-the-art gear and accessories. The new breed of ultralightweight stand bags — which can be comfortably carried or placed upright in a riding cart — feature carbon fiber legs and weigh as little as 2.5 pounds, with adjustable straps that help distribute the weight to ease back strain.

Pull carts remain a popular option, although they have been somewhat eclipsed by the rapid rise of power pushcarts. Sometimes called motorized pushcarts or electric caddies, these battery-powered carts feature remote and follow functions, where golfers can enjoy a hands-free experience, allowing the pushcart to stay with them step by step. Stewart Golf, a manufacturer of the carts, says its U.S. sales are nearly four times what they were before the pandemic.

None of this is to take away from those who still prefer riding around a golf course; after all, their ranks still dwarf the walkers. But those who like to hoof it say there's no comparison.

Tim Miles of Scottsdale had to use a cart for a couple of years for health reasons, and now that he's well enough to walk again, he has no intention of returning to riding. "There's something about guys walking down the middle of a fairway early in the morning with their bags on their back and chatting," he says. "I'm playing better golf. I'm not just driving across the course. Being able to walk a golf course again has changed my world. It went from black and white to full color."

Joe Passov is a writer in Scottsdale, Ariz. He can be reached at reports@wsj.com.

data.title: Bulletin from the Extraordinary General Meeting of Creturner Group 2025
data.description:
The shareholders of Creturner Group AB (publ) held an Extraordinary General Meeting on 8 April 2025 in Stockholm

The shareholders of Creturner Group AB (publ) held an Extraordinary General Meeting on 8 April 2025 in Stockholm and resolved on, inter alia, the following.

Resolution on election of new Auditor, Jenny Gentele from ABC Revision. The meeting was closed

Contact details

Creturner Group AB is a group with two business areas in environmental technology.

The digital part is a technical computing business that is expanding to strengthen its long-term potential and create a presence in the fast-growing market for high-performance computing (HPC) capacity. The company has its own facilities in Sweden with low-cost energy production to reduce raw material costs and environmental impact.

The environmental part offers corporate customers an efficient and permanent method to offset their activities. The method is based on BCR (Biochar Carbon Removal), which involves converting residual biomass into the element carbon and storing it to reduce the amount of carbon dioxide in the atmosphere. Corporate customers of Creturner can then purchase carbon offsets in the same way they traditionally offset for climate change, for example by planting trees. Unlike tree planting, Creturner's approach is immediate, permanent and clearly measurable. Monitoring is done through linked smart contracts that give the customer insight into the results and future financial return of the environmental investment. www.creturner.com

Creturner is listed on NGM Nordic SME in Stockholm and on the Stuttgart Stock Exchange in Germany.

data.title: Croatian shares rebound, Koncar soars 6.90%
data.description:
ZAGREB (Croatia), April 8 (SeeNews) - The Croatian equity market gained ground on Tuesday, after a four-day losing streak, mirroring a recovery on financial markets worldwide on prospects for trade deals to ease the impact of US President Donald Trump's new tariffs.

The 18-share equity benchmark, the CROBEX, advanced 3.83% to close at 3,158.30 points. The narrower CROBEX10 index added 3.69% to 1,998.60 points.

Index heavyweight Hrvatski Telekom [ZSE:HT] was the most traded company with a turnover of 1.4 million euro ($1.5 million), as its shares rose 0.71% to 42.70 euro.

Electrical concern Koncar [ZSE:KOEI], a major exporter, was the biggest daily gainer among the blue chips. Its shares went up 6.90% to 449 euro in a turnover of 763,000 euro.

The regular turnover fell to some 6.0 million euro from 9.5 million euro a day earlier. Besides it, in a 840,000 euro block deal a total of 2,000 shares of Koncar changed hands at a price of 420 euro apiece.

($ = 0.917 euro)

data.title: Wall Street Economists Expect Trump to Make Deals on Tariffs. They're Still Worried.
data.description:
By Sam Goldfarb

One thing to keep in mind as stocks rise Tuesday on hopes for tariff-reducing deals: Wall Street economists who are worried about a recession have already baked in assumptions that tariffs will be negotiated lower.

In an April 6 report, analysts at Goldman Sachs raised their 12-month recession odds to 45% from 35%. That was based on the assumption that the effective U.S. tariff rate rises by 15 percentage points.

If all of Trump's announced tariffs go into effect and aren't scaled back, the average tariff rate would rise by 20 percentage points, the Goldman analysts wrote. An increase of only 15 percentage points would "require a large reduction in the tariffs scheduled to take effect on April 9," they wrote.

Goldman's report was published before Trump threatened an additional 50% tariff on Chinese imports starting Wednesday if China doesn't withdraw retaliatory tariffs.

This item is part of a Wall Street Journal live coverage event. The full stream can be found by searching P/WSJL (WSJ Live Coverage).

data.title: Impact of Tariff Rises on U.K. Inflation Unclear, Says BOE's Lombardelli
data.description:
By Paul Hannon

Higher U.S. tariffs on imports from other countries will weaken U.K. economic activity, but the impact on inflation is less clear, Bank of England Deputy Governor Clare Lombardelli said Tuesday.

The BOE left its key interest rate unchanged in March, but investors expect policymakers to lower borrowing costs when they next meet in May.

As with other countries, U.K. exports of steel, aluminum and automobiles face higher U.S. tariffs. But the U.K. will face lower tariffs on other goods exports than the members of the European Union.

"The tariffs are likely to depress activity," Lombardelli said during a discussion on productivity growth. "On inflation, it depends more on the circumstances and how other countries will respond."

Lombardelli said policymakers will "bring all of that together" when they next meet in early May.

Write to Paul Hannon at paul.hannon@wsj.com

data.title: White House Press Secretary Levitt says tariffs on China have not gone into effect yet
data.description:
White House Press Secretary Levitt says tariffs on China have not gone into effect yet. The 104% cumulative tariffs on China will go into effect at midnight tonight (they have not gone into effect).

The initial report was hosted by Edward Lawrence from Fox news:

Meanwhile Apple is loading a 747 with 200,000 iPhones.

data.title: UN chief rejects new Israeli plan to control Gaza aid
data.description:
United Nations Secretary-General Antonio Guterres on Tuesday rejected a new Israeli proposal to control aid deliveries in Gaza, saying it risks "further controlling and callously limiting aid down to the last calorie and grain of flour."

"Let me be clear: We will not participate in any arrangement that does not fully respect the humanitarian principles: humanity, impartiality, independence and neutrality," Guterres told reporters.

No aid has been delivered to the Palestinian enclave of some 2.1 million people since March 2. Israel has said it would not allow the entry of all goods and supplies into Gaza until Palestinian militants Hamas release all remaining hostages.

COGAT, the Israeli military agency that coordinates aid, last week met with U.N. agencies and international aid groups and said it proposed "a structured monitoring and aid entry mechanism" for Gaza.

"The mechanism is designed to support aid organizations, enhance oversight and accountability, and ensure that assistance reaches the civilian population in need, rather than being diverted and stolen by Hamas," COGAT posted on X on Sunday.

Jonathan Whittall, the senior U.N. aid official for Gaza and the West Bank, said last week that there was no evidence of aid being diverted.

Israel last month resumed its bombardment of Gaza after a two-month truce and sent troops back into the enclave.

"Gaza is a killing field – and civilians are in an endless death loop," said Guterres as he again called for the immediate and unconditional release of all hostages, a permanent ceasefire, and full humanitarian access in Gaza.

"With crossing points into Gaza shut and aid blockaded, security is in shambles and our capacity to deliver has been strangled," he said.

"As the occupying power, Israel has unequivocal obligations under international law – including international humanitarian law and international human rights law," Guterres said.

That means Israel should facilitate relief programs and ensure food, medical care, hygiene and public-health standards in Gaza, he said. "None of that is happening today," he added.

Israel says it does not exercise effective control over Gaza and therefore is not an occupying power.

The war in Gaza was triggered on Oct. 7, 2023, when Hamas killed 1,200 people in southern Israel, and took some 250 hostages, according to Israeli tallies. Since then, more than 50,000 Palestinians have been killed, according to Gaza health authorities.

data.title: Nymex Overview: Petroleum Futures Up at Midday, Below Morning HighsOPIS
data.description:
Crude oil futures were higher near midday Tuesday in continued volatile trading that earlier left Brent and West Texas Intermediate futures in the red.

Petroleum markets may have found some support from equity markets that opened Tuesday's session with a strong rally. While stock indexes were higher at near midday, the gains were weakening some in brisk trading.

WTI volume over the last three days has averaged roughly 1.85 million contracts and trading volume on Monday topped 2 million lots for the first time since mid-January.

The NYMEX May WTI and June Brent contracts were slightly higher heading into the afternoon. The May WTI was up 39cts to $61.09/bbl just after noon ET and June Brent was 12cts higher at $64.33/bbl.

Gasoline and distillate contracts were up by 1ct to 2cts, leaving them a couple of cents below morning highs. The May ULSD was about 2cts higher at $2.0895/gal and the NYMEX May RBOB contract was 1.29cts higher at $2.033/gal.

Cash markets were mixed, despite the gains in futures. The San Francisco CARBOB market continued to cool off from earlier highs. East of the Rockies markets were mostly following futures, but wider discounts in Gulf Coast gasoline left prices there relatively flat.

data.title: Netflix Looks Insulated From a Trade War4 Reasons Why.
data.description:
By Adam Levine

Though it faces risk like any international firm these days, Netflix may be the tech company that can skate through a trade war with the least amount of damage.

In contrast to product-makers like Apple, down 16% since President Donald Trump announced his latest tariffs last week, Netflix is down 3%. The S&P 500 is off 9% over the same stretch.

Unlike many big tech companies, Netflix is integrated into local economies around the world. Punishing the streaming platform may turn out to be unattractive.

Netflix still faces the same larger threat as everyone else — that a trade war will sink the global economy into a deep recession. On Monday at the Economic Club of New York, BlackRock CEO Larry Fink said that "most CEOs I talk to would say we are probably in a recession right now."

Netflix would likely see some subscription loss in a global recession, but inexpensive entertainment has traditionally survived and even flourished in economic downturns. The Great Depression was considered by many to be the "Golden Age of Hollywood."

"Staying home watching Netflix is definitely a cheaper way to spend the night than going out to a restaurant or going to a venue," Oppenheimer analyst Jason Helfstein told Barron's.

But investors have to also consider the threat of retaliation in an escalating trade war. U.S. goods are squarely in the crosshairs of other countries, and countermeasures may include what is known as "digital service taxes." Netflix already faces these in several countries, along with additional requirements to invest in local film production. The taxes are generally in the range of 2% to 5% of revenue. In a trade war, foreign countries could raise these rates, while other nations could impose new ones.

But subscription-based Netflix may get different treatment from ad-based offerings like Alphabet's Google Search and Meta's Facebook. High taxes on advertising may make their way to consumer prices, but it is hidden to them as one cost among many — and thus more politically expedient.

With subscriptions, though, a digital tax would result in direct price increases, and Netflix would surely communicate the reason for the hike. That makes digital subscription taxes a domestic political liability.

Countries could also impose new local investment requirements, but such a move could actually protect Netflix, which already spends significantly in local economies. From 2020 to 2023, according to Bernstein analyst Laurent Yoon, Netflix invested $6.8 billion in Europe and maintained 11 local offices and production hubs.

Netflix also licenses a lot of foreign content, according to Yoon; 60% of all titles on Netflix are from non-U.S. sources. "No other platform comes close to what Netflix does," Yoon said. These licensing fees to local producers are another reason countries may be wary of punishing Netflix.

Netflix could even be protected from international boycotts of U.S. products. The company is the number one streamer in almost every market where it competes, and it will be hard to replace it with local alternatives. Even in France, where the government expends a lot of effort protecting local culture, French Canal Plus has a far smaller subscriber base than Netflix, according to Yoon.

Write to Adam Levine at adam.levine@barrons.com

data.title: Gasoline margins rise
data.description:
Northwest European gasoline refining profit margins rose by a little over $1 a barrel on Tuesday, as April exports out of Europe are set for a rise month-on-month.

  • A total of 10,000 metric tons of Eurobob E5 traded, with BP, Gunvor and Vitol selling to Litasco and Musket.

  • Just 1,000 tons of Eurobob E10 traded with Phillips 66 selling to BP.

  • Wider gasoline-naphtha spreads are opening up various arbitrage opportunities for gasoline blenders in ARA, according to Sparta Commodities' Philip Jones-Lux, including to Mexico and Canada that would usually be serviced by the U.S. Gulf coast refining hub.

  • EU and UK gasoline and blending component exports to other regions are set to jump to 1.14 million barrels per day in April, up from 860,000 bpd in March, Kpler data shows.

  • Venezuela's state-run oil company PDVSA is restarting its El Palito refinery's fluid catalytic cracker, which remained shut for 11 months, to partially offset the suspension of key units at its largest refinery due to a blackout, sources close to its operations said on Tuesday.​
